one_liner: Identifies key trends in healthcare claims data (spend, utilization, unit cost) and the drivers behind them, across BETOS/CPT hierarchies.
summary: Healthcare Trends (HCT) helps identify key trends in a large healthcare claims dataset and understand the drivers responsible for those trends. Analysis flows from BETOS Category/Subcategory down through BETOS Family to CPT code, using Specialty (SPEC), Place of Service (POS), and Diagnosis (ICD) as supporting dimensions. It powers Payment Policy Insight articles, ad-hoc trend analysis, and Cotiviti Client Conference content, typically framed as year-over-year (YoY) spend-share, utilization, and unit-cost movement.
use_when: User asks about healthcare spend/utilization/unit-cost TRENDS over time (YoY, rolling 12 months)
User asks which BETOS categories, subcategories, families, or CPT codes drive a change
User asks about drivers by specialty, place of service, or diagnosis (ICD) group
User asks about behavioral health, telehealth, or medical-drug claim trends
User asks which medical policies drive savings or how edit rates are changing
User asks a payment-policy or claims-trend analytical question
do_not_use_when: User asks about sales opportunities, whitespace, or ACV -> use WSI
User asks about membership/enrollment market size -> use AIS
User asks to benchmark a client against peers -> use Peer
User asks about Cotiviti's geographic presence -> use Footprint
User asks about single-customer payment-variance/footprint claims outcomes -> use Unidata
sample_questions: Which BETOS categories and subcategories show the largest YoY change in spend share, and how do utilization and unit-cost trends explain it?
Within the flagged BETOS subcategory, which line of business has the biggest YoY shift in spend share?
Which specialty groups are the top contributors to the category/subcategory trend?
Which places of service are most responsible for the observed movement in spend share?
Which diagnosis (ICD) groups are emerging as key drivers of the trend?
For the top drivers, which medical policies account for the highest share of savings, and how are edit rates changing YoY?
